title: Similar triangles
source: Essential Mathematics for the Australian Curriculum, Year 9 (Cambridge, Greenwood et al. 2024) — Chapter 7 Geometry, Section 7G. Images are direct crops of the original textbook pages.

workedExamples.title: Example 15 — Choosing a similarity test for triangles
Example 16 — Finding a missing length using similarity

questions.topic: identifying corresponding vertices, sides and angles in similar triangles
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): E

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: matching up vertices in the wrong order in a similarity statement (e.g. writing ΔABC ||| ΔHGI instead of ΔABC ||| ΔGHI)
misconceptions.reason: the order of letters in a similarity statement must match corresponding vertices - matching the wrong pairs makes every follow-up ratio wrong

questions.topic: finding the scale factor between two similar right-angled triangles
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 2.5

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: dividing the sides in the wrong order when finding the scale factor, or multiplying by the scale factor instead of dividing (or vice versa)
misconceptions.reason: scale factor = corresponding side on the larger/enlarged triangle divided by the matching side on the smaller triangle - getting this upside down inverts every answer

questions.topic: recalling the tests for similarity
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): SAS, AAA, and RHS

questions.topic: recalling the tests for similarity
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): shape, size

questions.topic: choosing the correct similarity test (SSS, SAS, AAA or RHS)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): AAA

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: picking SSS when only two sides are known, or picking SAS when the equal angle isn't the INCLUDED angle between the two known sides
misconceptions.reason: SSS needs all three sides in ratio; SAS needs the angle between the two matched sides specifically - an angle elsewhere in the triangle doesn't count

questions.topic: choosing the correct similarity test (SSS, SAS, AAA or RHS)

misconceptions.reason: SSS needs all three sides in ratio; SAS needs the angle between the two matched sides specifically - an angle elsewhere in the triangle doesn't count

misconceptions.reason: SSS needs all three sides in ratio; SAS needs the angle between the two matched sides specifically - an angle elsewhere in the triangle doesn't count

questions.topic: identifying the triangle that is not similar to the other two
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ΔDEF

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: assuming two triangles are similar just because they look alike, without checking angles or side ratios
misconceptions.reason: similarity must be proven with one of the four tests (SSS, SAS, AAA, RHS) - visual resemblance is not proof

questions.topic: giving reasons why two triangles are similar
questions.answer: ∠ACB = 25° (angle sum of a triangle), so ΔABC ||| ΔHGI (AAA)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠ACB = 25° (angle sum of a triangle), so ΔABC ||| ΔHGI (AAA)

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: picking SSS when only two sides are known, or picking SAS when the equal angle isn't the INCLUDED angle between the two known sides
misconceptions.reason: SSS needs all three sides in ratio; SAS needs the angle between the two matched sides specifically - an angle elsewhere in the triangle doesn't count

questions.topic: giving reasons why two triangles are NOT similar
questions.answer: ∠WXY = 55° (angle sum of a triangle), not similar as the angles are not equal
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): ∠WXY = 55° (angle sum of a triangle), not similar as the angles are not equal

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: assuming two triangles are similar just because they look alike, without checking angles or side ratios
misconceptions.reason: similarity must be proven with one of the four tests (SSS, SAS, AAA, RHS) - visual resemblance is not proof

questions.topic: explaining why triangles formed by intersecting transversals across parallel lines are similar
questions.answer: 2 pairs of equal alternate angles are always formed (vertically opposite angles are also equal), so the triangles are similar by AAA
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 2 pairs of equal alternate angles are always formed (vertically opposite angles are also equal), so the triangles are similar by AAA

questions.topic: comparing the AAS congruence test with the closest similarity test
questions.answer: AAA - in congruency a side length is needed for the triangles to be the same size; in similarity it is not needed
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): AAA - in congruency a side length is needed for the triangles to be the same size; in similarity it is not needed

questions.topic: completing a table of length, area and volume scale factors for similar prisms
questions.answer: Length scale factor: Original 1, Image 1 = 2, Image 2 = 3, Image 3 = 4. Area (cross-section): Original 4, Image 1 = 16, Image 2 = 36, Image 3 = 64. Area scale factor: Original 1, Image 1 = 4, Image 2 = 9, Image 3 = 16. Volume: Original 12, Image 1 = 96, Image 2 = 324, Image 3 = 768. Volume scale factor: Original 1, Image 1 = 8, Image 2 = 27, Image 3 = 64.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): Length scale factor: Original 1, Image 1 = 2, Image 2 = 3, Image 3 = 4. Area (cross-section): Original 4, Image 1 = 16, Image 2 = 36, Image 3 = 64. Area scale factor: Original 1, Image 1 = 4, Image 2 = 9, Image 3 = 16. Volume: Original 12, Image 1 = 96, Image 2 = 324, Image 3 = 768. Volume scale factor: Original 1, Image 1 = 8, Image 2 = 27, Image 3 = 64.

questions.topic: spotting the relationship between area scale factor and length scale factor
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): Area scale factor = (length scale factor)²

questions.topic: generalising the area scale factor rule
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): n²

questions.topic: applying the area scale factor rule
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): i  100   ii  400   iii  10 000

questions.topic: applying the area scale factor rule to a fractional length scale factor
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 1/4

questions.topic: spotting the relationship between volume scale factor and length scale factor
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): volume scale factor = (length scale factor)³

questions.topic: generalising the volume scale factor rule
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): n³

questions.topic: applying the volume scale factor rule
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): i  125   ii  1000   iii  1/8
